html:
Alimak Group AB Thesis

Alimak is not the sub-SEK 1 billion distressed microcap implied by the draft data. The live primary listing is Nasdaq Stockholm ALIG, with the London 0R8W line reflecting the same Swedish equity. The stock is around SEK 106, with roughly 106 million shares, so market cap is about SEK 11.2 billion, not USD 96 million. The setup is still interesting, but it is a quality-industrial rerating thesis, not a “2x earnings with net cash” thesis.

The variant view is that Alimak is being marked as a construction-cycle loser after falling from a 52-week high of SEK 173.60 to roughly SEK 106, while the company is actually a diversified vertical-access platform with service, industrial, wind, facade-access refurbishment, safety products, and M&A optionality. Q1 2026 was not pretty, but it was not thesis-breaking: revenue fell 5% reported but rose 3% organically, book-to-bill stayed above 1, and adjusted EBITA margin was 16.7% in a weak construction market (Q1 report).

The core misunderstanding is mix. Construction is weak, especially hoists in Europe and North America, but Construction was only SEK 346 million of Q1 revenue and SEK 40 million of EBITA. Industrial, Wind, Facade Access, and HSPS together are the real company. Industrial did a 22.5% EBITA margin in Q1 despite a temporary mix issue. Wind revenue grew 22% reported and 32% at constant currency with a 20.3% EBITA margin. Facade Access is seeing book-to-bill above 1 and refurbishment/replacement demand. Service revenue was SEK 626 million in Q1, about 38% of group revenue, and the company explicitly says service demand is more tied to the installed base than new equipment cycles (market overview).

The forward valuation is reasonable today and cheap if management is even mostly right. At SEK 106, the stock trades around 16 to 17x 2026 EPS if one uses the post-Q1 analyst estimate near SEK 6.39, and closer to 13 to 14x on some forward data sets. That is not dirt cheap on a depressed year, but it becomes cheap on 2027 to 2028 earnings power. Management’s New Heights 2.0 targets are 8% to 12% average annual revenue growth and a 20% adjusted EBITA margin by 2028 (targets). On a 2025 revenue base near SEK 6.9 billion, even the low end implies SEK 8.6 billion-plus revenue in 2028. At 19% to 20% EBITA margins, that is SEK 1.6 to 1.8 billion of EBITA versus a current EV around SEK 13.6 billion.

This is a self-help and mix-upgrade story hiding inside a machinery label. New Heights already took revenue from SEK 3.8 billion to about SEK 7 billion and adjusted EBITA margin from 10.6% to 17.4% over five years (strategy). The next leg is less about “construction comes back” and more about aftermarket performance, product lifecycle management, technology partnerships, geographic expansion, and bolt-on M&A. Management also refinanced debt into long-term facilities maturing in 2029, keeping leverage at 1.85x net debt/EBITDA versus a target below 2.5x. This is not net cash, but the balance sheet is usable.

The near-term negative is obvious and well known: Q1 EPS was down 20%, cash flow was weak, construction orders were down 25%, HSPS was soft, and currency shaved EBITA. That is why the stock is near the bottom of the 52-week range and below recent analyst targets, including a SEK 141 consensus noted after Q1 by Simply Wall St and a broader one-year target average around SEK 160 on Fintel. The important point is that the market is not paying for the 2028 plan. It is paying for a low-growth, mid-cycle industrial with construction drag.

The upside torque comes from three things happening at once: construction merely stops getting worse, Wind and Industrial keep compounding, and the market begins to underwrite 18% to 20% margins as normal rather than peak. If 2028 EPS lands around SEK 9.50 to SEK 11.50, a 16x to 18x multiple is not aggressive for a niche industrial with recurring service, high ROCE excluding goodwill, dividend policy of 40% to 60% of net profit, and M&A capacity. That math gets the stock well above current sell-side targets.

Retail context is also useful: this is not crowded. The London line is thin, U.S. retail attention is negligible, and even Swedish retail chatter appears modest relative to larger industrial names. There is no meme premium, no AI narrative premium, and no obvious forced-buyer crowd. The opportunity is old-fashioned estimate compounding plus multiple repair.

The bear case is that construction weakness lasts, Industrial mix does not normalize, service growth underwhelms, and M&A consumes balance-sheet capacity without improving growth. In that world, EPS stagnates near SEK 5.50 to SEK 6.00, the market values Alimak as a cyclical machinery stock, and the dividend is the main return. The thesis is wrong if service revenue turns structurally negative or if the 20% margin target starts looking promotional rather than operationally grounded.

Bear target: SEK 82, about 23% downside from SEK 106. Construction stays weak through 2027, EBITA margin compresses to 15% to 16%, EPS is stuck near SEK 5.50, and the market applies a depressed 15x P/E.

Base target: SEK 155, about 46% upside. Revenue grows low to mid single digits near term, Construction stabilizes, Industrial and Wind carry mix, 2028 EBITA reaches roughly SEK 1.6 billion, and the stock trades around 16x normalized EPS or about 10x to 11x EBITA.

Bull target: SEK 225, about 112% upside. Management gets close to New Heights 2.0, revenue approaches SEK 9 billion, adjusted EBITA margin reaches 20%, EPS moves above SEK 11, leverage falls, and the market values Alimak as a service-backed niche industrial rather than a construction-equipment cyclic.
